svg.text: FRCS Part 1 (General Surgery) · UK practice (NICE CG189 · BOMSS · NHS England) · pathway, referral, procedures & complications
%EWL = percentage excess weight loss (approximate, varies between series). Band now rarely offered on the NHS.
stepped care · Tier 3 is the usual gateway to Tier 4
universal / public-health prevention — healthy-eating & physical-activity promotion, national campaigns
lifestyle / community weight management — multicomponent behavioural programmes (often 12 weeks; incl. commercial providers)
specialist MDT medical service — physician, specialist nurse, dietitian, psychologist, physiotherapist; intensive medical management
incl. GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide / tirzepatide) and low-calorie / low-energy diets
specialist bariatric & metabolic surgery — surgery with peri-operative MDT and long-term follow-up
balance surgical risk against long-term benefit
for MDT assessment if BMI ≥40, or 35–39.9 with a significant condition improvable by weight loss (T2DM, hypertension, OSA) —
and the person agrees to lifelong annual follow-up
reduce the BMI threshold by 2.5 kg/m² for South Asian, Chinese, other Asian, Middle Eastern, Black African or
African-Caribbean backgrounds (higher risk at lower BMI)
OFFER if BMI ≥35 + recent-onset (≤10 yr) T2DM; CONSIDER if BMI 30–34.9 + recent-onset T2DM (lower still for high-risk
patients no longer need to have completed all non-surgical measures first, nor be pre-judged fit for anaesthesia before
referral — the specialist service assesses this
MDT + psychological assessment; optimise comorbidities; smoking cessation; pre-op liver-shrinkage (low-calorie) diet;
silicone band around the upper stomach
creates a small pouch; inflated / deflated
~75–80% of the greater curve is stapled and
removed, leaving a tubular stomach; reduces
at incisura; de novo / worsening GORD &
small gastric pouch anastomosed to a Roux
CT can be normal); dumping; nutritional
tachycardia (>120) is often the ONLY early sign — leak
until proven otherwise; sepsis; low threshold for
leading cause of early post-op death — mechanical +
iron, vitamin B12, folate, calcium, vitamin D, thiamine
persistent vomiting → Wernicke’s; give IV thiamine BEFORE
rapid weight loss; consider ursodeoxycholic acid
Tachycardia (HR >120) after any bariatric operation = anastomotic / staple-line leak until proven otherwise — don’t wait for peritonism.
Persistent vomiting post-bariatric → give IV thiamine BEFORE glucose to prevent Wernicke’s encephalopathy.
Internal hernia after RYGB: intermittent colicky pain; CT can be normal — have a low threshold for diagnostic laparoscopy.
Avoid NSAIDs (and smoking) after RYGB — marginal ulcer / perforation risk.
Band causing dysphagia / reflux / regurgitation → deflate the band (aspirate the port) urgently.
Rapid weight loss predisposes to gallstones; lifelong supplements + annual bloods are mandatory, not optional.
Sources: NICE CG189 Obesity (2014, updated 2023) · NICE PH46 (ethnicity BMI thresholds) · BOMSS nutritional guidance · NHS England four-tier commissioning model.
%EWL figures are approximate and vary between series. The adjustable gastric band is now rarely offered on the NHS.
Revision aid — verify against current guidance before clinical use.
Revision resources & realistic mock exams · mockfrcs.co.uk
